behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: The risk is module handoff (file discovery -> filename parsing/sorting -> execution order), which unit tests can miss. Integration is enough because this behavior text does not require real OS/network/subprocess contracts or external API response-shape checks. Rejected System: the behavior only states ordering by prefix, not a required external contract/round-trip side-effect proof beyond in-process module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order prefixed `.sql` files, run the runner against a test DB/fake executor wired through real discovery + ordering modules, and assert execution happened in ascending prefix order.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This is idempotency across runs via interaction between migration execution and `_migrations` state tracking; that cross-module state handoff is beyond pure unit logic. Integration with real repository/metadata access catches it. Rejected System: behavior text does not explicitly require real external contract validation beyond local DB/module interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice on the same test DB with one migration that has an observable effect; verify first run applies it and records it, second run skips execution and leaves effect unchanged while `_migrations` still contains one record.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Failure handling depends on interaction among transaction wrapper, executor, and run-loop stop logic; integration is needed beyond unit tests. Rejected System: although rollback is DB behavior, the behavior text does not explicitly require real external response-contract checks or OS/subprocess conditions; a local integration DB test provides the needed reality signal.
behaviors.test_description: Use two migration files where the first contains a failing statement after a prior write and the second would make another detectable change. Run once and assert: first migration effects are absent (rollback), second migration was not attempted, and failed migration is not marked applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires concurrent execution from separate processes, which depends on real subprocess/DB locking timing and externally visible exactly-once side effects. This triggers System (real infra + round-trip verification of single-application outcome).
behaviors.test_description: Launch two real runner processes simultaneously against the same DB and migration set, wait for both to finish, then read back DB state and `_migrations` to prove each migration was applied exactly once total (no duplicates, no misses).

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is initialization ordering across modules (bootstrap metadata table -> apply files), so unit-only tests can miss orchestration bugs. Integration is sufficient. Rejected System: behavior text specifies ordering on first run, not real external contract/error-shape checks or subprocess/OS behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a fresh test DB lacking `_migrations`, run the runner with at least one migration, and assert `_migrations` is created and usable before migration application is recorded/applied (verified by resulting table/data and metadata rows).
